name: propertree

metadata.name: propertree
metadata.summary: Library to help create meaningful structures from yaml or json.

documentation:
# Propertree

Map Python data structures to classes and create tree of resolvable objects.

## Overview

This library provides a set of classes that can be used to take Python data
structures and map them to a set of properties organised in a tree-like
structure. The basic idea is that a Python object with one or more attributes
can be represented by a unique key in a dictionary whereby the the value of
that key represents the internal makeup of the object i.e. attributes etc.

The basic design is as follows:

A ```PTreeSection``` object takes Python data structures dict/list/literal
after having been converted from YAML or JSON. This input is treated like a
tree of properties whereby each level of a structure equates to a branch
containing a set of "property overrides" which are identified by a unique
root key that maps to an implementation of ```PTreeOverrideBase```. Descendant
lists, dictionaries or unknown literals are treated new branches.

Once the whole tree has been mapped, objects can be retreived by either
iterating over the root object or accessing them directly as attributes.

For example lets say we have the following YAML:

```
config:
  fire:
    danger:
      level: high
  banana:
    danger:
      level: low
```

And the following accompanying Python code:

```
from propertree.propertree2 import PTreeOverrideBase, PTreeSection

class Config(PTreeOverrideBase):
  override_keys = ['config']


root = PTreeSection(MYYAML)
```

We can then access the config as follows:

```
print(root.config.fire.danger.level)
print(root.config.banana.danger.level)
```

## Property Inheritance

Property inheritance is supported by passing down all properties identified at
a branch level to all descendent branches. This allows property objects to
access any property within it's call chain although it would be the most
recently overriden version if one exists. For example:

```
input: /etc/foo
checks:
  chk1:
    input: /etc/bar
    condition: C1
  chk2:
    condition: C2
```

In the above, ```checks.chk1.input``` would be "/etc/bar" whereas ```checks.chk2.input``` would be "/etc/foo".


## Mapped Properties

It is possible to compose complex properties made up of one or more "member"
properties. These are called *mapped* properties and are provided by the
```PTreeMappedOverrideBase``` class. A mapped property has two parts; a *primary*
and its *members*. A property can only be a member of one mapped property (i.e.
be associated with a single primary). These properties also have the special
feature that allow them to be defined either *explicitly* using their
full construct i.e. primary and members or *implicitly* using just their
members. If the latter form is used, the primary is implicitly created such
that when the members are accessed it is always done through the primary.

For example, here is some code to define a mapped property:

```
from propertree.propertree2 import PTreeOverrideBase, PTreeMappedOverrideBase, PTreeSection

class MapPrimary(PTreeMappedOverrideBase):
  override_keys = ['mapprimary']
  override_members = ['member1']


class Member1(PTreeOverrideBase):
  override_keys = ['member1']

...
```

the following will both behave the same when accessed:

The explicit declaration:

```
mapprimary:
  member1:
    attr1:
```

And the counterpart implicit declaration:

```
member1:
  attr1:
```

Are both accessed as follows:

```
mapprimary.member1.attr1
```

## Logical Groupings

Sometimes it might be useful to represent properties or content within a
property as a logical function. To achieve this, propertree has builtin
logical operator properties which map to the ```PTreeLogicalGrouping``` class.
This class provides a default implementation of logical operations that can be
used by any property. Grouped items are expected to have a ```result```
attribute that returns a boolean result such that the respective logical
operator of that grouping is then applied to the set of all results.

In the following example we have a top-level operator with two items, each of
which is itself an operator, the first having two property items and second
having one. This equates to AND(OR(P1, P2), NOT(P3)).

```
and:
  or: [P1, P2]
  not: P3
```

Example code for this would look like:

```
from propertree.propertree2 import PTreeSection

root = PTreeSection(MYYAML)
result = root.and.result
```

methods.name: tests.unit.test_ptree_mapped_properties.TestPTreeMappedProperties.test_mapping_single_member_full
documentation:
A single fully defined mapped property i.e. the principle property name
is used rather than just its member(s).

methods.name: tests.unit.test_ptree_mapped_properties.TestPTreeMappedProperties.test_mapping_single_member_short
documentation:
A single lazily defined mapped property i.e. the member property names
are used rather than the principle.

methods.name: tests.unit.test_ptree_mapped_properties.TestPTreeMappedProperties.test_mapping_list_members_partial
documentation:
A list of lazily defined properties. One with only a subset of members
defined.

methods.name: tests.unit.test_ptree_mapped_properties.TestPTreeMappedProperties.test_mapping_list_members_full
documentation: A list of lazily defined properties. Both with all members defined.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.MappedMemberPrimaryConflict
documentation:
Exception raised when a property is found to be a member of more than one
primary.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.RegistryConflictError
documentation:
Exception raised to indicate that a property has already been
registered for a given key name.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.InvalidBranchPathError
documentation: Raised when a branch is referenced with an invalid path.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertyIsNotAMember
documentation:
Raised when a mapping tries to access a property as a member when that
property is not a member.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertreeTreeError
documentation: Generic error for propertree errors.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry.post_registration_tasks
documentation:
Auto-registration order is random and nothing stops a key
having properties registered against it more than once. This is a
cleanup to ensure each registration looks as it should. The last
property registered against a key takes precedence over the rest.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry._ensure_not_registered
documentation:
Ensure that the given key has not already been registered and raise
an RegistryConflictError if it has.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry._register
documentation:
Register the given property class against all keys it has defined and
if necessary link to any mappin primaries it maybe associated with.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry.register
documentation:
Register override/property classes.

@param to_register: a list of overrides to register.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry._unregister
documentation: Unregister a property (and it's keys) from the registry.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideRegistry.unregister
documentation:
Unregister override/property classes.

@param to_unregister: a list of overrides to unregister.

The override registry contains registrations of properties to keys and is
then used later to resolve properties as they are discovered in a tree.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideMeta

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.OverrideMeta.migrate_protected_attrs
documentation:
In order to make the changes necessary to switch private attributes to
public while not breaking any code that is using these attributes we
allow grace period where we map old format attributes to new.

This will be removed in a future release.
Used to automatically register property classes on module load.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase.get_override_keys_back_compat
documentation:
To support backwards compatibility with propertree.py we support old
and new ways of defining keys.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase.__init__
propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase.override_name
documentation: This is the key name used to register this override.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase.override_path
documentation: This is the full resolve path for this override object.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideBase._len_query
documentation:
The way we query the size/length of a property differs depending
on the type of property so this supports the different ways to
query that information.
Base class for all property implementations.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideLiteralType

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideLiteralType.has_valid_type
documentation: Ensure the content has a type that we support.
Property for literal types.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeLogicalGrouping

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeLogicalGrouping.and_stop_on_first_false
documentation:
By default we do not process items in an AND group beyond the first
False result since they will not change the final result. Some
implementations may want to override this behaviour.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeLogicalGrouping.or_stop_on_first_true
documentation:
By default we do not process items in an OR group beyond the first
True result since they will not change the final result. Some
implementations may want to override this behaviour.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeLogicalGrouping.fetch_item_result
documentation:
By default we expect each item to be an object with a 'result'
attribute. Override this method if this is not the case.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeLogicalGrouping.result
documentation:
Get the result of each item in the group and apply the chosen logical
operator to the group. Returns the final result after applying the
operator.

First class override for logical groupings. This will only take effect when
used as a property attribute i.e. it cannot be used as a property in its
own right.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeMappedOverrideBase

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeMappedOverrideBase.__iter__
documentation: Generator giving all items of this property.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeMappedOverrideBase.members
documentation: Generator giving all member properties of this mapping.
Base class for implementations of mapped properties.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.State

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.State.__init__
propertree.propertree2.State.get_ref
documentation:
A dissociated reference to this state object that can be modified
without impacting the original state data.
Representation of property state.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PropQuery

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PropQuery.__init__
propertree.propertree2.PropQuery.validate_groupings
documentation:
NOTE: stacked properties can contain items from different groupings
      (group ids).
Property query object. Provides a common way to query for properties.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.BuildInfo

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.BuildInfo.__init__

documentation: Settings for building a property object.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertyCache
documentation: Cache for built property objects.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertyStateManager

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertyStateManager.build_stack
documentation: Build/instantiate each item in a property stack.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PropertyStateManager.deepcopy
documentation:
Make a deepcopy of properties and set all items is_copy=True to mark
them as global. This can be overridden by descendants who want to
modify with their own values.

Stack for property state objects providing methods to query and
build property objects.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager.flatten_path
documentation:
Since mapping primaries and logical groupings are registered at the
same branch property level we need to flatten their full path when
searching for them since that is how they are registered.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager.make_all_branch_properties
documentation: Supports properties from branches. Does not support flat structure.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager._mark_new_branch
documentation:
Each new branch is marked as a leaf until it gets a branch of its own
at which point it, and all of its ancestors marked as non-leaf.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager._get_member_override_primary
documentation:
If the property at path is a member of a PTreeMappedOverrideBase
return primary class otherwise return None.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager._add_implicit_member_primary
documentation: Create an implicit primary property for the member at member_path.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager._ensure_member_primary
documentation:
Ensure that if this a mapping member, that it's primary has been
registered. If it has not this would imply that it is an implicit
primary.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager.check_switch_for_mapping_logical_group_cls
documentation:
If we are a PTreeLogicalGrouping that is part of a mapping, find the
mapping primary to see if it has a customer grouping type and switch
it out if it does.

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeOverrideManager._get_override_handler
documentation: We expect the topmost registered class to be the one that is used.
Manages a property tree.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.BranchInfo
documentation: Holds information about a branch.

classes.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeSection

methods.name: propertree.propertree2.PTreeSection.__init__

documentation: The representation of the property tree.
